sections.heading: 0. Frame + source
sections.body: Subject: distil how MCP structures an agent-friendly interface and apply the transferable PRINCIPLES (not the protocol) to the lume CLI. Grounded in the MCP spec, concept page 'Tools' (modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools, spec rev 2025-06-18), fetched this iteration. MCP is the teacher; lume stays dependency-free (no MCP server, no JSON-RPC, no SDK). No engine code this iteration - discovery.json + forks only.

sections.heading: 1. How MCP structures an agent interface (from the spec)
sections.body:
- Discovery: clients call `tools/list` to enumerate every capability; no out-of-band knowledge needed.
- Self-description: each tool is a record with `name` (unique id), `title` (human label), `description`, `inputSchema` (a JSON Schema for arguments), optional `outputSchema` (JSON Schema for the result), and `annotations` (behaviour hints).
- Uniform invocation: every capability is called the same way - `tools/call` with `{name, arguments}` - args are a named object validated against inputSchema.
- Structured results: results carry `structuredContent` (a JSON object, validated against outputSchema when present) AND a text block for back-compat; `isError` flags execution failure.
- Two-tier errors: PROTOCOL errors (unknown tool, invalid arguments, server error) as JSON-RPC `{code, message}`; vs EXECUTION errors returned in the result with `isError: true`. The agent can tell 'I called it wrong' from 'it ran and failed'.
- (Protocol machinery not relevant to a CLI: JSON-RPC transport, capability negotiation/initialize, listChanged notifications, multi-modal content blocks, resources/prompts.)

sections.heading: 2. Principles checklist (lume-specific, each testable)
sections.body:
P1 Discoverable: an agent can enumerate every verb the CLI offers, machine-readably, with no prior knowledge.
P2 Self-describing: for each verb, the agent can fetch what it does and what arguments it takes (names, required/optional, types) - the CLI analogue of inputSchema.
P3 Schema-fetchable data: every data entity has a fetchable schema (lume already has this: `schema <entity>`).
P4 Uniform invocation grammar: verbs and their arguments follow one predictable pattern (verb, then named flags / positional in a consistent order) - no per-verb surprises.
P5 Machine-readable output: every verb can emit a structured, parseable result (not only human prose), consistently shaped.
P6 Structured, two-tier errors: failures are machine-parseable and distinguish bad-invocation (usage) from operation-failed (gate/domain), with stable codes - lume already separates these by EXIT CODE (2 vs 1); the gap is a parseable error payload.
P7 Deterministic + self-contained: all of the above hold with no LLM and no new dependency.

sections.heading: 3. lume CLI audit against the checklist
sections.body:
P1 Discoverable - PARTIAL. `entities` lists data kinds and `schema`/`get` exist, but there is NO machine-readable listing of the VERBS themselves; only a human USAGE string in cli.py. An agent can't enumerate commands without parsing prose.
P2 Self-describing verbs - GAP. Verb arguments live only in usage strings ('lume plan add [-t type] [-g tag] "<sketch>"'); no fetchable per-verb arg description/schema (no inputSchema analogue).
P3 Schema-fetchable data - COMPLIANT. `lume entities` + `lume schema <entity>` cover all 8 entities; `get` returns validated JSON.
P4 Uniform grammar - MOSTLY. Verb-first with -w/-t/-c/-g flags is consistent; rough edges: positional shapes vary (new=slug+title, reject=reason, decide=decision[+rationale], plan add/link sub-verbs), and the recently-added `get plan` alias hints at ad hoc naming.
P5 Machine-readable output - GAP (biggest). Read verbs are structured (`get`/`schema` emit JSON, `entities` line-per-item), but MUTATING/▸status verbs print human prose to stdout ('opened iteration 001, phase proposed: ...'; status queue/detail). No `--json` and no structured result object for new/open/transitions/decide/plan/snapshot/status.
P6 Structured errors - PARTIAL. Exit codes are a real strength and already two-tier (2 = usage, 1 = domain/gate, 0 = ok), but error bodies are plain strings on stderr ('lume: ...') with no machine field/code.
P7 Deterministic + self-contained - COMPLIANT and must stay so.

sections.heading: 4. Gap classification (adopt / reject / defer)
sections.body:
ADOPT - Verb discovery surface (P1): a machine-readable listing of verbs (e.g. `lume verbs` or `lume help --json`) - the tools/list analogue. Cheap, high agent value, stdlib-only.
ADOPT - Structured output for all verbs (P5): a consistent JSON result object (a `--json` global flag, or structured-by-default for machine callers). Highest leverage; the main thing stopping an agent from driving lume without scraping prose.
ADOPT - Structured errors (P6): emit a parseable error object (code + message) while keeping the human string and exit codes. Small, complements the exit-code strength.
DEFER - Per-verb input schema (P2): describing each verb's args as data (inputSchema analogue) is valuable but larger; depends on the output/discovery shape landing first. Revisit in planning once P1/P5 exist.
DEFER/NARROW - Grammar standardisation (P4): only the rough edges; must not break existing callers/tests. Treat as a careful, test-guarded cleanup, not a redesign.
REJECT - JSON-RPC transport, capability negotiation, listChanged notifications, multi-modal content blocks, resources/prompts, annotations: protocol machinery irrelevant to a CLI and/or dependency-laden. Fails dependency-free + buy-back. (lume's stdout/stderr + exit codes ARE its transport.)

sections.heading: 5. Open forks for planning
sections.body:
F1 Verb-discovery shape: a new `lume verbs` vs `lume help --json` vs extend `entities` to also cover verbs vs a `lume describe <verb>`.
F2 Structured output mechanism: a global `--json` flag (human default) vs structured-by-default with a `--human`/text mode; and whether each verb gets an outputSchema. Back-compat with current human stdout is the constraint (tests + the operator read it).
F3 Structured-error shape: a JSON error object on stderr under --json (code + message + maybe the offending field); keep exit codes as the primary signal; codes as data.
F4 Per-verb input schema (P2): represent verb args as schemas; how many verbs, and how to avoid freezing the positional grammar prematurely.
F5 Grammar cleanups (P4): enumerate the specific inconsistencies worth fixing vs leaving; each must be test-guarded and caller-safe.
F6 v1 scope: discovery recommends P1 (verb listing) + P5 (structured output) + P6 (structured errors) as the high-value core; P2/P4 likely deferred. Planning ratifies the cut.

sections.heading: 6. Scope held / deferred
sections.body: No engine code changed this iteration; the only artifact is this discovery.json (no discovery.md - JSON-only model). MCP adoption / any protocol server is explicitly out of scope and rejected (dependency-free). Deferred to planning: ratifying which principles to adopt (recommended core: verb discovery, structured output, structured errors) and sizing them into reviewable execution slices; P2 (per-verb input schema) and P4 (grammar cleanup) deferred unless planning pulls them in.
